title: How to Use Intuition When Choosing a Therapist
meta_description: Trust your gut to find the right therapist. Learn how to use intuition, not just online profiles, to choose a healer who truly fits.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Choosing a Therapist
og_description: Trust your gut to find the right therapist. Learn how to use intuition, not just online profiles, to choose a healer who truly fits.
body:
You Already Know Who’s Right for You - You Just Don’t Trust It Yet

Let me guess. You’ve been thinking about seeing a therapist for a while. Maybe months. Maybe years. You’ve scrolled through a dozen profiles on Psychology Today. You’ve read bios that all sound the same - “I use a trauma-informed, client-centered approach.” You’ve felt that weird pit in your stomach when you try to make a decision. And you still haven’t picked up the phone.

That pit? That’s your intuition. It’s not indecision. It’s your nervous system saying, “I’m not sure this is safe yet.” And honestly? That’s a good thing.

Most people choose a therapist the way they choose a new brand of toothpaste - based on location, insurance, and a few star ratings. But you’re not most people. If you’re reading this, you’re someone who feels things deeply. You’ve been told your whole life that you’re “too sensitive” or “overthinking it.” Nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most. That means you need to choose a therapist differently - with your whole body, not just your head.

So let’s talk about how to actually use your intuition when picking someone to sit across from you in that little room. Because your gut knows. It’s just been drowned out by noise. Right?!

“Your intuition isn’t telling you who the ‘best’ therapist is. It’s telling you who you can actually be honest with. That’s a very different thing.”

Why Your Brain Will Lie to You About This Choice

Here’s what happens when you try to pick a therapist using logic alone. You make a list. You check credentials. You look at years of experience. You read about their “specialties” - anxiety, depression, trauma, relationship issues. And you think, “Well, they check all the boxes. I should book with them.”

But then you get on the first call or sit down in the chair, and something feels… off. You can’t name it. They’re nice. They’re qualified. But you don’t feel safe. You don’t feel seen. And you end up spending the whole session performing - talking about what you think you’re supposed to talk about instead of what’s actually eating you alive.

That’s your brain failing you. Your brain is a pattern-matching machine. It looks for evidence that matches the checklist. But your body knows things your brain can’t articulate. Your body feels the energy in the room. It senses whether this person can actually hold your pain or if they’re going to flinch when you get real.

So stop treating this like a hiring decision. You’re not looking for the most qualified candidate. You’re looking for someone whose nervous system can meet yours. That’s a whole different kind of search.

The First Clue Is in Their Bio - But Not the Way You Think

I want you to try something. Open up a therapist directory or a clinic website. Read the bios, but don’t read them for content. Read them for tone. What does the language feel like in your body? Does it feel warm? Clinical? Distant? Does it sound like someone who’s done a lot of training or someone who’s actually lived a little?

You’re looking for a match in frequency, not in expertise. A bio that uses words like “cultivating resilience” and “evidence-based modalities” might hit your head as smart, but does it hit your heart? Does it make you feel like this person would get it if you told them you sometimes feel like you’re not even real?

Here’s a trick. Read the bio out loud. Not in your head - out loud. How does your throat feel? Tight? Relaxed? If your throat tightens, that’s a red flag. Your body is saying, “I’d have to edit myself with this person.” If your shoulders drop even a little, that’s a green light.

I’m not saying ignore credentials. I’m saying put them second. Let your body filter first. You can always check if they’re licensed later. But you can’t unfeel that initial mismatch.

If you want a resource to help you tune into these body signals more clearly, check out The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It’s not a light read, but it’ll show you exactly why your body is screaming at you when your brain is trying to be polite. See on Amazon (paid link)

The 30-Second Gut Check Before You Book

Before you even call or email, do this. Look at their photo. I know, photos can be misleading. But they’re also a doorway. Look into their eyes in the photo. What do you feel? Does it feel like they’d be able to look at you without making it weird? Does it feel like they have their own stuff sorted out, or do they look like they need a therapist too?

Trust that first flash of feeling. It’s not judgment. It’s data. Your intuitive system is picking up on micro-expressions, posture, even the lighting in the photo. If something feels slightly off - not critical, just off - honor that. You’re not being shallow. You’re being sensitive to energetic information that your conscious mind can’t process yet.

I once had a client who said she chose her therapist because the therapist was wearing a purple scarf in her photo. “I don’t know why,” she said. “It just felt like she had a soul.” That’s intuition. It’s not logical. It’s accurate.

If you’re struggling to trust these flashes because your mind keeps overriding them, read The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It’s about safety and intuition, and it’ll show you how your gut has been right all along - you just stopped listening. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Pay Attention to on the First Call

Most therapists offer a free 15-minute consultation call. This is your biggest opportunity to use your intuition. But you have to stop treating it like an interview and start treating it like a first date - where you’re paying attention to how they make you feel, not just what they say.

Here are the specific things to tune into:

  • How do they answer the phone? Do they sound present or rushed? Do they say your name like they’re reading it off a list or like they’re meeting you?
  • Do they let you lead, or do they fill the silence? A therapist who can’t handle a few seconds of quiet on a consultation call probably can’t handle your real silence in session.
  • How do you feel in your chest? Warm? Open? Tight? Like you want to hang up? That’s your answer.
  • Do they ask you what you’re looking for, or do they tell you what they offer? The best ones ask. The ones who lecture you about their approach are already showing you who’s in charge.

And here’s the thing - you don’t have to explain yourself. If you get a weird feeling, you don’t owe them an explanation. You can just say, “Thank you, I’ll think about it,” and move on. Your intuition doesn’t need to justify itself to anyone. Does that land?

What If You’re Too Sensitive to Judge This?

I hear this a lot. “But Kalesh, I’m so sensitive that I get a bad feeling about everyone. How do I know if it’s intuition or just my anxiety?”

Fair question. Here’s how you tell the difference.

Anxiety feels like static. It’s scattered, repetitive, and it loops. You think, “What if they’re not good? What if I waste money? What if I get triggered and they can’t handle it?” That’s fear talking. It’s trying to keep you safe by keeping you stuck.

Intuition feels like a clear, single note. It’s not panicked. It’s just certain. It might say, “This one’s not right,” without a long list of reasons. It doesn’t need to convince you. It just knows.

If you’re in doubt, sit with the feeling for 24 hours. Don’t make a decision. Just let the feeling sit. Anxiety fades or gets louder. Intuition stays the same - steady, quiet, patient.

If you’re still not sure, try this exercise. Close your eyes. Imagine yourself sitting in a room with this therapist. Imagine telling them the thing you’re most ashamed of - the thing you’ve never told anyone. How does your body feel? If you feel even a little bit of relief, that’s a yes. If you feel more shame or fear, that’s a no.

Your body knows if it’s safe to be vulnerable with someone. Your brain will talk you into anything. Trust your body.

When You’re in the Room - Trust the First Session

You’ve booked the session. You’re sitting in the waiting room or staring at the Zoom screen. Now what?

Pay attention to the first five minutes. That’s when your intuition is loudest. After that, your brain starts editing, people-pleasing, and trying to make a good impression. But those first five minutes? That’s raw data.

Ask yourself:

  • Do I feel like I can breathe in this room?
  • Do I feel like I have to be careful with my words?
  • Do I feel like they’re actually seeing me, or are they scanning me for a diagnosis?
  • Do I feel more relaxed or more tense than when I walked in?

If you feel worse after the session than before, that’s not “good therapy.” That’s a mismatch. Good therapy might stir things up, but it shouldn’t leave you feeling more alone or more broken. It should leave you feeling like someone finally gets it.

I had a therapist once who spent the whole first session talking about herself. I knew in the first ten minutes it was wrong. But I stayed for three months because I thought I was being too picky. Don’t do that. Trust the first impression. It’s almost never wrong.

If you want a deeper approach for trusting those early impressions, read How to Do the Work by Dr. Nicole LePera. She talks a lot about nervous system matching and how to know if someone is actually safe for you. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Red Flags Your Intuition Is Already Picking Up

Let’s name some things that your gut might be sensing but your brain is dismissing:

  • They interrupt you. Even once. If they can’t let you finish a thought in a consultation, they won’t in session.
  • They use too much jargon. “I use a psychodynamic approach to address your attachment wounds.” Translation: they’re hiding behind training because they’re not comfortable being human with you.
  • They don’t ask questions. If they just talk at you about their approach without asking what you need, they’re not curious about you.
  • You feel like you have to educate them. If you’re explaining your identity, your culture, or your experience to them, and they’re not already informed, that’s a sign they’re not doing their own work.
  • They seem uncomfortable with your emotions. You start crying, and they hand you a tissue immediately instead of letting you feel it. Or they change the subject. That’s a sign they can’t hold space for your pain.

Your intuition is picking up on all of this. Trust it. You don’t need a reason to say no. You just need to say no.

What If You’ve Already Started With the Wrong Therapist?

You’re not stuck. You’re allowed to leave. Even if you’ve been seeing them for six months. Even if they’re “nice.” Even if you feel guilty.

Staying with the wrong therapist is worse than having no therapist. It teaches your nervous system that even in the place where you’re supposed to be safe, you have to perform. That’s retraumatizing. That’s not healing.

If you’re feeling like it’s not a fit, trust that. You don’t need a dramatic breakup. You can just say, “I’ve decided to take a different direction. Thank you for your time.” That’s it.

Your intuition didn’t fail you. It was telling you from the start. You just weren’t ready to listen yet. Now you are.

If you need help navigating the messy process of ending with a therapist and finding a better one, Maybe You Should Talk to Someone by Lori Gottlieb is a great read. It’s honest about the therapist-client relationship from both sides. See on Amazon (paid link)

A Simple Practice to Strengthen Your Intuition for This (and Everything)

Your intuition is a muscle. If you’ve been ignoring it for years, it’s weak. But you can strengthen it. Here’s a practice that takes five minutes.

Every morning, before you check your phone, ask yourself one question: “What do I need today?” Don’t think. Just feel. The first thing that comes - even if it’s “I need to pee” - that’s your intuition talking. Honor it. Act on it.

Then, throughout the day, practice making small decisions with your gut. What to eat for lunch. Which route to walk. Whether to say yes or no to a coffee date. The more you trust it on small things, the louder it gets on big things - like choosing a therapist.

You already know who’s right for you. You already know who isn’t. The only thing standing between you and the right therapist is the belief that you don’t know. But you do. You always have.

So take a breath. Open that directory again. But this time, don’t read with your eyes. Read with your chest. Your gut. Your whole body. Let it guide you. It’s been waiting for you to listen.

And when you find that person - the one who feels like a soft place to land - you’ll wonder why you ever doubted yourself.

You’ve got this.

Want to go deeper into trusting your intuition? Take our free assessment to see where your intuitive sensitivity is strongest and where it needs support. Start here or try our intuition quiz to get clarity on your next step.
